my_sentence: I have a good day today.
correction: I had a good day today.
explanation: Use past tense when describing the completed day. The teacher immediately supplied 'had.'
correct_examples: I had a good day today.

my_sentence: He had a many TV commercial.
correction: He had many TV commercials.
explanation: Do not use 'a' before 'many,' and use a plural count noun after 'many.'
correct_examples: He had many TV commercials.

my_sentence: I watched some of movies that he directed.
correction: I watched some movies that he directed.
explanation: Use 'some movies' without 'of' unless a specific set follows, such as 'some of his movies.'
correct_examples: I watched some of his movies.

my_sentence: I can't remember detail.
correction: I can't remember the details.
explanation: Use the plural 'details' for multiple facts about an event.
correct_examples: I can't remember the details.

my_sentence: There is a very famous woman celebrity.
correction: There is a very famous female celebrity.
explanation: The natural noun phrase is 'female celebrity.' The teacher explicitly supplied it.
correct_examples: She is a famous female celebrity.

my_sentence: She manager an air condition company.
correction: She manages an air-conditioning company.
explanation: The singular subject 'she' needs the present-tense verb 'manages.' 'Air-conditioning' modifies 'company.'
correct_examples: She manages an air-conditioning company.

my_sentence: She appeared on TV for her production.
correction: She appeared on TV to advertise her products.
explanation: Use plural 'products' for the different goods and an infinitive to express purpose.
correct_examples: She appeared on TV to advertise her products.

my_sentence: We have some home shopping channel.
correction: We have some home-shopping channels.
explanation: After 'some,' use the plural count noun 'channels.'
correct_examples: We have some home-shopping channels.

my_sentence: I never bought anything from here.
correction: I have never bought anything from a home-shopping channel.
explanation: Use the present perfect for an experience up to now. The teacher framed the question with 'have never bought.'
correct_examples: I have never bought anything from that channel.

my_sentence: You can get tomorrow or today.
correction: You can get them today or tomorrow.
explanation: A transitive 'get' needs the object 'them,' followed by the time expression.
correct_examples: You can get them tomorrow.

my_sentence: I can see this.
correction: I can see it.
explanation: Use 'it' for the shared screen already being discussed.
correct_examples: I can see it clearly.

my_sentence: These things really happen nowadays.
correction: These things rarely happen nowadays.
explanation: The intended adverb was 'rarely,' meaning not often, rather than 'really.' The teacher explicitly corrected it.
correct_examples: These mistakes rarely happen now.

my_sentence: We can change price, use our system.
correction: We can use our system to change prices.
explanation: Use 'use + object + infinitive' to express the method and purpose.
correct_examples: We use the system to change prices.

my_sentence: The manager need to check their tasks.
correction: The manager needs to check their tasks.
explanation: The singular subject 'manager' takes 'needs.' The teacher prompted the final -s.
correct_examples: The manager needs to check the work.

my_sentence: Most of them can't get a commission.
correction: Most of them can't get commissions.
explanation: Use plural 'commissions' when discussing repeated payments generally. The teacher supplied the plural.
correct_examples: Most clerks don't get commissions.

my_sentence: The store just need to recruit some hourly workers.
correction: The store just needs to recruit some hourly workers.
explanation: The singular subject 'store' requires 'needs.'
correct_examples: The store needs to recruit workers.

my_sentence: Like eating at a restaurant or buy some furniture.
correction: Like eating at a restaurant or buying some furniture.
explanation: Use parallel gerunds after 'like.' The teacher explicitly prompted 'buying.'
correct_examples: Like eating out or buying furniture.

my_sentence: The company need to pay you a lot.
correction: The company needs to pay you more.
explanation: The singular subject 'company' takes 'needs.'
correct_examples: The company needs to pay a bonus.

my_sentence: There are too many people drive their cars on the highway.
correction: There are too many people driving their cars on the highway.
explanation: Use an -ing participle after the noun to describe what the people are doing.
correct_examples: There are too many people driving today.

my_sentence: The traffic will be very terrible.
correction: The traffic will be very heavy.
explanation: The natural collocation for serious congestion is 'heavy traffic.' The teacher supplied it.
correct_examples: Traffic will be heavy during the holiday.

my_sentence: I just need to wait until the traffic become normal.
correction: I just need to wait until the traffic becomes normal.
explanation: The singular subject 'traffic' takes 'becomes.'
correct_examples: Wait until traffic becomes normal.

my_sentence: We have 13 day for holiday in a year.
correction: We have 13 holidays in a year.
explanation: Use the plural count noun 'holidays' after a number. The teacher explicitly recast the phrase.
correct_examples: We have 13 holidays a year.

my_sentence: It's a new year, so we celebrate for it.
correction: It's a new year, so we celebrate it.
explanation: The transitive verb 'celebrate' takes a direct object without 'for.'
correct_examples: We celebrate the New Year.

my_sentence: A hospital need to have some nurses and doctors.
correction: A hospital needs to have nurses and doctors.
explanation: The singular subject 'a hospital' requires 'needs.' The teacher prompted the singular form.
correct_examples: A hospital needs doctors and nurses.

my_sentence: Doctors are applied for.
correction: They usually apply for jobs as doctors.
explanation: Applicants perform the action, so use active 'apply,' not passive 'are applied.' The teacher explicitly corrected the verb.
correct_examples: Graduates apply for jobs as doctors.

my_sentence: They usually apply for the doctor.
correction: They usually apply for positions in the medical field as doctors or nurses.
explanation: Apply 'for' a position, work 'in' a field, and serve 'as' a doctor or nurse.
correct_examples: They apply for positions in the medical field.

my_sentence: They can choose designer.
correction: They can choose to be designers.
explanation: Use 'choose to be' before a profession and plural 'designers' for multiple graduates.
correct_examples: They can choose to be designers.

my_sentence: They can becoming a cook.
correction: They can become cooks. / They can focus on being cooks.
explanation: After modal 'can,' use base 'become'; after 'focus on,' use gerund 'being.'
correct_examples: They can become cooks.

my_sentence: You need to relationships with many people.
correction: You need to build relationships with many people.
explanation: Use the collocation 'build relationships.' The teacher supplied the verb.
correct_examples: Sellers need to build relationships.

my_sentence: You need to be trusted by others.
correction: You need to earn their trust.
explanation: The teacher supplied the concise collocation 'earn their trust.'
correct_examples: A seller needs to earn customers' trust.

my_sentence: I haven't tried to.
correction: I haven't tried.
explanation: Do not leave the infinitive marker 'to' without a following verb. The teacher explicitly removed it.
correct_examples: I haven't tried.
context: Whether Jack has worked as an online seller.

my_sentence: Their tasks are relaxed and easy.
correction: Their tasks are relaxing and easy.
explanation: Things are 'relaxing'; people feel 'relaxed.' The teacher explained the contrast.
correct_examples: The tasks are relaxing.

my_sentence: I will have enough time to read book.
correction: I will have enough time to read books.
explanation: Use plural 'books' when speaking generally. The teacher explicitly prompted it.
correct_examples: I have time to read books.

my_sentence: I can find a good academic atmosphere.
correction: I can find a good atmosphere.
explanation: The teacher asked Jack to use the broader, natural phrase 'a good atmosphere' in this context.
correct_examples: The library has a good atmosphere.

my_sentence: I can focus on study.
correction: I can focus on studying.
explanation: After the preposition 'on,' use the gerund 'studying.' The teacher supplied it.
correct_examples: I can focus on studying.

my_sentence: There are many students who is studying, so I can easily affected by them.
correction: There are many students who are studying, so I can be easily affected by them.
explanation: The plural relative subject needs 'are,' and the passive verb needs 'be affected.'
correct_examples: Students who are studying can motivate me.
context: Being influenced by other library users.

my_sentence: It can make me to keep focusing on my study.
correction: It can make me focus on my studies.
explanation: After causative 'make,' use the bare infinitive 'focus.' Use plural 'studies' for academic work.
correct_examples: The atmosphere makes me focus on my studies.

my_sentence: It can make me feel relax.
correction: It can make me feel relaxed.
explanation: After 'feel,' use the adjective 'relaxed,' not the base verb 'relax.'
correct_examples: Reading makes me feel relaxed.

my_sentence: They can read some books in their free time.
correction: They can read some books during their free time.
explanation: The teacher supplied 'during' for the time period.
correct_examples: They read during their free time.

my_sentence: Two hundred years ago, we don't have any medicine to make our life longer.
correction: Two hundred years ago, we didn't have medicine to make our lives longer.
explanation: A finished past period needs 'didn't have,' and multiple people have plural 'lives.' The teacher explicitly prompted both.
correct_examples: People didn't have modern medicine then.

my_sentence: I read some simple version about these books.
correction: I read some simple versions of these books.
explanation: After 'some,' use plural 'versions'; use 'of' to connect versions to the books.
correct_examples: I read simplified versions of these books.
context: Books by Sun Tzu and Confucius.

my_sentence: I'm not interested about them.
correction: I'm not interested in them.
explanation: The adjective 'interested' takes the preposition 'in.' The teacher explicitly corrected it.
correct_examples: I'm not interested in those books.

my_sentence: I went to there just to visit.
correction: I went there just to visit.
explanation: Do not use 'to' before the place adverb 'there.' The teacher recast the phrase.
correct_examples: I went there to visit.

my_sentence: It become normal after I rest.
correction: It becomes normal after I rest.
explanation: The singular subject 'it' takes 'becomes.' The teacher explicitly supplied the form.
correct_examples: My eyes become normal after I rest.

my_sentence: I give you a wrong example.
correction: I gave you a wrong example.
explanation: The example was already given, so use past-tense 'gave.' The teacher explicitly corrected it.
correct_examples: I gave you the wrong example.
context: Correcting the description of red eyes.

my_sentence: I need to rest five to ten minutes.
correction: I need to rest for five to ten minutes.
explanation: Use 'for' before a duration. The teacher supplied the complete phrase.
correct_examples: I rest for ten minutes.

my_sentence: I need to have a dinner during that time.
correction: I need to have dinner during that time.
explanation: Meals normally take no article in the fixed expression 'have dinner.' The teacher removed 'a.'
correct_examples: I need to have dinner at six.

my_sentence: The tasks and the routine task are repetitive.
correction: The tasks are repetitive.
explanation: Use one clear plural subject with plural agreement. The teacher simplified the repeated noun phrase.
correct_examples: The librarian's tasks are repetitive.

my_sentence: There are many volunteers will help you organize books.
correction: There are many volunteers who can help you organize books.
explanation: Use the relative pronoun 'who' to connect 'volunteers' to the verb clause. The teacher explicitly prompted it.
correct_examples: There are volunteers who can help.

my_sentence: You just need to answer some question.
correction: You just need to answer some questions.
explanation: After 'some,' use plural 'questions.'
correct_examples: Librarians answer some questions.

my_sentence: I'm curious about being librarian.
correction: I'm curious about being a librarian.
explanation: A singular profession noun needs the article 'a.' The teacher supplied the phrase.
correct_examples: I'm curious about being a librarian.
context: Considering library work in the future.
